comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the concept of using LLMs and automated agents for research tasks such as bug fixing and hyperparameter optimization. While some commenters appreciate the approach as a useful engineering tool, others express skepticism about its novelty and cost-effectiveness, noting it is more trial-and-error than true innovation.

comments.author: n_bhavikatti
comments.text: The temperature clamp fix and "Optuna++" actions by the agents (the cause of basically all improvement to eCLIP) indicate they are good at finding bugs and hyper-parameter tuning. But when it comes to anything beyond that, such as novel architectural shifts, agents aren't good enough. With no clear path forward they tend to randomly change things, which is a poor approach. Agents: Optimization >> innovation
comments.analysis: This comment accurately distinguishes between optimization capabilities and true innovation, reflecting a nuanced understanding of the limitations of current automated research agents.

comments.author: carlsborg
comments.text:
> “ The agent acted like a hyperparameter optimization algorithm with some basic reasoning baked in.”

Good lens.

The crux of the auto research repo is basically one file - program.md which is a system prompt that can be summarized as “do this in a loop: improve train.py, run the training, run evals, record result. Favor simplicity”. The other files are an arbitrary ML model that is being trained.
comments.analysis: Provides a clear and accurate summary of the core mechanism behind the autoresearch approach, showing insight into the implementation details.

comments.author: _pdp_
comments.text:
Take some working code. Ask an LLM to fix bugs. Measure performance and test coverage. Feed the results back into the LLM. Repeat.

This has been the standard approach for more complex LLM deployments for a while now in our shop.

Using different models across iterations is also something I've found useful in my own experiments. It's like getting a fresh pair of eyes.
comments.analysis: Describes a practical iterative approach to using LLMs in software development that aligns well with known deployment strategies.

comments.author: lucasay
comments.text: This feels less like automated research and more like structured trial and error with a decent feedback loop. Still useful, but I think the real bottleneck is how good your eval metric is. If that’s weak, the whole loop just optimizes for the wrong thing faster.
comments.analysis: Insightfully points out that the quality of evaluation metrics critically affects the usefulness of automated optimization loops.

comments.author: dvt
comments.text:
Ok, so looking at the commit log[1], I was mostly interested in seeing what the "moonshot ideas" implementations looked like, but basically everything is just hyperparameter tuning. Which is nice, but likely not worth the $$$ spent on the tokens. Am I missing something here?

[1] https://github.com/ykumards/eCLIP/commits/main/autoresearch
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about cost-effectiveness and the limited scope of the approach, highlighting practical considerations.

comments.author: datsci_est_2015
comments.text:
I often use LLMs to explore prior art and maybe find some alternative ways of thinking of problems. About 90% of what it tells me is useless or inapplicable to my domain due to a technicality it could not have known, but the other 10% is nice and has helped me learn some great new things.

I can’t imagine letting an agent try everything that the LLM chatbot had recommended ($$$). Often coming up in recommendations are very poorly maintained / niche libraries that have quite a lot of content written about them but what I can only imagine is very limited use in real production environments.

On the other hand, we have domain expert “consultants” in our leadership’s ears making equally absurd recommendations that we constantly have to disprove. Maybe an agent can occupy those consultants and let us do our work in peace.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced perspective on the utility and limitations of LLMs in research, emphasizing partial usefulness and potential organizational impact.

comments.author: lamroger
comments.text:
Awesome breakdown! It really feels like a hyper-hyper parameter search + bug fixer.

I started looking at Kaggle again and autoresearch seems to converge to many of the solution vibes there.

Wild ensembles, squeezing a bit of loss out. More engineering than research IMO
comments.analysis: Concise evaluation that aligns with other comments about the approach being more about engineering optimization than novel research.

comments.author: jpcompartir
comments.text:
There are better techniques for hyper-parameter optimisation, right? I fear I have missed something important, why has Autoresearch blown up so much?

The bottleneck in AI/ML/DL is always data (volume & quality) or compute.

Does/can Autoresearch help improve large-scale datasets? Is it more compute efficien than humans?
comments.analysis: Raises important questions about the comparative advantage and practical impact of the autoresearch approach.

comments.author: pikachu0625
comments.text: It's better to outsource optimization phases. Our idea should be for constraint, assumptions etc. for breakthrough. Boyd often argues that once you can express a problem in a standard mathematical form, the implementation becomes a commodity that software can handle automatically.
comments.analysis: Offers a strategic viewpoint emphasizing that automation should handle routine tasks while human insight drives innovation.

comments.author: love2read
comments.text: So... It did work. It found bugs (that he didn't know about) and it did optimization (that he hadn't done).
comments.analysis: A brief but positive endorsement of the approach's practical utility, though lacking depth.

comments.author: motbus3
comments.text: I've done something with a small project I have and I had very similar results overall.
comments.analysis: Personal anecdote that supports the general findings but adds little analytical value.
Provides useful information about dataset availability but is somewhat tangential to the main discussion.

comments.author: mlmonkey
comments.text:
> Then I lock down Claude Code’s permissions to only edit these two files and run run.sh. No direct Python execution, no pip installs, no network access, no git push, etc.

How does one run Claude Code without network access?
comments.analysis: Raises a practical question but does not contribute to the core analysis of the autoresearch approach.

comments.author: the_arun
comments.text: Try this if the main link is not responsive - https://archive.is/6xLiU
comments.analysis: A simple workaround for link availability, unrelated to the technical discussion.

comments.author: saidnooneever
comments.text:
pretty cool experiment, i thought about someone maybe doing this and am happy you did it in this way. nice writeup too. this made me giggle a bit: "At one point it got tired of waiting for training to finish and just ended the conversation. I wouldn’t give it full autonomy just yet :)"

thanks for sharing your results and the road to them!
comments.analysis: Expresses positive sentiment and humor but adds no substantive critique or insight.

comments.author: BrokenCogs
comments.text: Does autoresearch work for projects that are not llm based? Eg in karpathy's example he is optimizing the nanogpt. What if I wanted to improve a Unet for image segmentation?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question but does not provide analysis or answer.
